natural_occurrence.description: The potassium salt of sorbic acid. Named after rowan berries (Sorbus aucuparia) from which sorbic acid was first isolated in 1859. While natural sorbic acid exists in rowan berries at about 0.1%, virtually all commercial potassium sorbate is produced synthetically. Most widely used food preservative globally.

manufacturing.description: Produced by neutralizing synthetically manufactured sorbic acid with potassium hydroxide or potassium carbonate. Sorbic acid is synthesized through condensation of crotonaldehyde with ketene. Potassium salt is preferred over sodium salt due to better solubility and lack of genotoxic concerns.

other_uses.cosmetics: Widely used as a preservative in cosmetics and personal care products
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Used in oral medications and topical preparations

eu.conditions: Maximum levels typically 200-2000 mg/kg depending on food type

usa.notes: Recognized as Generally Recognized as Safe when used appropriately

japan.notes: Most widely used preservative in Japan; often preferred over sorbic acid due to better water solubility

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0–25 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for sorbic acid and sorbates, expressed as sorbic acid)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: 25 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for sorbic acid and sorbates)

regulatory_history.event: Sorbic acid first isolated from rowan berries by A.W. von Hofmann
Antimicrobial properties of sorbic acid discovered
JECFA establishes group ADI of 25 mg/kg bw/day for sorbic acid and sorbates
EFSA comprehensive re-evaluation confirms safety; maintains group ADI

equivalents.product: glasses of wine (150ml treated with Campden tablets)

adi_context.source_note: Based on JECFA/EFSA group ADI for sorbic acid and sorbates. Potassium sorbate is considered one of the safest and most effective food preservatives available.
